M. M. Holton
P. Gokulakrishnan
M. S. Klassen
R. J. Roby
Combustion Science and Engineering, Inc.,
8940 Old Annapolis Road, Suite L,
Columbia, MD 21045
G. S. Jackson
Department of Mechanical Engineering,
University of Maryland,
College Park, Maryland 20742
Autoignition Delay Time
Measurements of Methane,
Ethane, and Propane Pure Fuels
and Methane-Based Fuel Blends
Autoignition delay experiments in air have been performed in an atmospheric ﬂow reac-
tor using typical natural gas components, namely, methane, ethane, and propane. Autoi-
gnition delay measurements were also made for binary fuel mixtures of methane/ethane
and methane/propane, and ternary mixtures of methane/ethane/propane. The effect of
CO2 addition to the methane-based fuel blends on autoignition delay times was also
investigated. Equivalence ratios for the experiments ranged between 0.5 and 1.25, and
temperatures ranged from 930 K to 1140 K. Consistent with past studies, increasing
equivalence ratio and increasing inlet temperatures over these ranges decreased autoi-
gnition delay times. Furthermore, addition of 5–10% ethane or propane decreased au-
toignition delay time of the binary methane-based fuel by 30–50%. Further addition of
either ethane or propane showed less signiﬁcant reduction of autoignition delays. Addi-
tion of 5–10% CO2 slightly decreased the autoignition delay times of methane fuel mix-
tures. Arrhenius correlations were used to derive activation energies for the ignition of
the pure fuels and their mixtures. Results show a reduction in activation energies at the
higher temperatures studied, which suggests a change in ignition chemistry at very high
temperatures. Measurements show relatively good agreement with predictions from a
detailed kinetics mechanism, speciﬁcally developed to model ignition chemistry of C1-C3
alkanes. DOI: 10.1115/1.4000590
1

text:
Introduction
Natural gas is a common fuel used in gas turbines for power
generation 1. It is becoming increasingly advantageous for in-
dustrial gas turbines to be capable of operating on a variety of fuel
blends while maintaining performance criteria such as low emis-
sions and ﬂame stability 2,3. The fuels of interest are liqueﬁed
natural gases from all over the world, coal-derived syngas, bio-
mass, landﬁll gas, and process gas. These fuels have a broad range
of compositions, which were shown to have signiﬁcant effects on
combustor performance, affecting efﬁciency, stability, and emis-
sions 2. Natural gas composition varies globally and, to a lesser
extent, within the United States. U.S. natural gas composition can
contain methane concentrations as low as 74.5% by volume and
ethane and propane concentrations as high as 13.3% and 23.7%
by volume, respectively, with the balance made up of higher-
order hydrocarbons, carbon dioxide, oxygen, nitrogen, and hydro-
gen sulﬁde 4–6.
Even a few percent of higher-order hydrocarbon was shown to
signiﬁcantly promote the ignition of methane-based fuels 5. This
is of serious concern in premixed systems such as industrial dry,
low emission DLE gas turbines, which often use aerodynamic

Introduction
Natural gas is a common fuel used in gas turbines for power
generation 1. It is becoming increasingly advantageous for in-
dustrial gas turbines to be capable of operating on a variety of fuel
blends while maintaining performance criteria such as low emis-
sions and ﬂame stability 2,3. The fuels of interest are liqueﬁed
natural gases from all over the world, coal-derived syngas, bio-
mass, landﬁll gas, and process gas. These fuels have a broad range
of compositions, which were shown to have signiﬁcant effects on
combustor performance, affecting efﬁciency, stability, and emis-
sions 2. Natural gas composition varies globally and, to a lesser
extent, within the United States. U.S. natural gas composition can
contain methane concentrations as low as 74.5% by volume and
ethane and propane concentrations as high as 13.3% and 23.7%
by volume, respectively, with the balance made up of higher-
order hydrocarbons, carbon dioxide, oxygen, nitrogen, and hydro-
gen sulﬁde 4–6.
Even a few percent of higher-order hydrocarbon was shown to
signiﬁcantly promote the ignition of methane-based fuels 5. This
is of serious concern in premixed systems such as industrial dry,
low emission DLE gas turbines, which often use aerodynamic

title: Combustion Science and Engineering, Inc.,
text:
methods to stabilize the ﬂame in a particular zone. Variations in
autoignition phenomena, based on fuel composition, could result
in ﬂashback and ﬂame stabilization in unintended locations, such
as the premixer, causing performance problems and hardware
damage 7. Researchers recognized the need to quantify the pro-
motional effects of higher-order hydrocarbons on natural gas ig-
nition and combustion processes 8. Autoignition delay measure-
ments for natural gas components and mixtures provide valuable
insight into the effects of additives on methane ignition, as well as
validation data for chemical kinetics mechanisms for predicting
ignition of methane-based fuels.
Recent studies showed that natural gas ignition chemistry can
be suitably represented by methane/ethane /propane mixtures
9,10. The current study was established to provide reliable au-
toignition delay time measurements of methane/ethane/propane
pure fuels and mixtures at relevant gas turbine inlet temperatures.
The measurements extend the current database of available igni-
tion data for natural gas fuels and provide much needed validation
for the development of kinetics models in this parameter space.
Pure methane oxidation has been extensively studied experi-
mentally for a number of years. A summary of the ignition delay
time measurements made up to 1969, mostly focusing on tempera-
tures above 1400 K and pressures near atmospheric, is provided
by Spadaccini and Colket 7. In recent years, the parameter space
for methane autoignition was well investigated for pressures up to
300 atm and temperatures ranging from 849 K to 1600 K
5,11–17. Pure ethane and propane oxidation has been less exten-
sively studied. An excellent summary of experimental ethane au-
toignition delay time measurements is provided by de Vries et al.
18. Measurements exist for temperatures ranging from 1218 K
to 1860 K and pressures up to 3 atm 18,19 for ethane oxidation,
and temperatures ranging from 900 K to 2211 K and pressures up
to 30 atm for propane oxidation 14,20–23. Figure 1 summarizes
the autoignition delay times for methane, ethane, and propane
pure fuels reported in the literature, showing the range of condi-
tions investigated by various researchers.
de Vries et al. 16 provided a summary of experimental studies
to date, which have investigated the effects of higher-order hydro-
carbons on methane ignition behavior. Goy et al. 12 studied the
effect of signiﬁcant 15% by volume additions of ethane and
propane on methane autoignition delay for temperatures ranging
from 952 K to 1428 K and pressures up to 20 atm. Signiﬁcant
changes in activation energy for the fuel blends were observed,

which were not captured by available kinetics models. Petersen et
al. 1 conducted autoignition delay time experiments for
methane/propane fuel blends for pressures up to 25 atm and tem-
peratures ranging from 1091 K to 1655 K, resulting in the modi-
ﬁcation of some reaction parameters in the GRI 3.0 mechanism.
Huang and Bushe 9 studied the effect of ethane and propane on
methane autoignition for temperatures ranging from 900 K to
1400 K and high pressures of 16–40 atm. Signiﬁcant promotional
effects of ethane and propane on methane autoignition were ob-
served. Antonovski et al. 24 collected autoignition delay times
of several fuel mixtures composed of methane, ethane, and pro-
pane for pressure conditions up to 58 atm and temperatures rang-
ing from 1032 K to 1577 K. Antonovski et al. 24 found ethane
and propane concentration to have a signiﬁcant inﬂuence on the
autoignition of methane. Petersen et al. 5 investigated the effect
of signiﬁcant 10% by volume additions of ethane and propane
on methane autoignition delay time for temperatures ranging from
1090 K to 2001 K and pressures up to 30 atm. Again, signiﬁcant
reductions in autoignition delay times were observed for the mix-
tures compared with pure methane measurements. de Vries et al.
16 collected experimental autoignition delay time data for meth-
ane blends containing up to 50% ethane and 25% propane for
temperatures ranging from 811 K to 1107 K and pressures around
20 atm. Healy et al. 25 continued this research and studied mix-
tures of methane and propane of ratios 90/10% and 70/30% at
temperatures ranging from 740 K to 1550 K and pressures of 10
atm, 20 atm, and 30 atm. The authors observed a negative tem-
perature coefﬁcient for autoignition delay time in the temperature
regime studied. A kinetics model developed by Petersen et al. 1
predicted the results of the autoignition delay experiments well,
capturing the negative temperature coefﬁcient trend. Bourque et
al. 26 continued the work of this group and studied the ignition
of natural gas blends of methane, ethane, propane, n-butane, and
n-pentane. The authors reported excellent agreement with the ki-
netics model 1, especially for fuel-rich mixtures at higher pres-
sures. They recommend further investigation of the effect of
higher up to 40% concentrations of ethane in the fuel blend, as
the model needs further validation in this parameter space. Figure
2 presents selected data sets for methane fuel blends containing
ethane and propane reported in the literature, showing the range of
conditions investigated and autoignition delay times measured by
the various researchers.
Very limited data exist for the atmospheric pressure and tem-
perature range studied herein. Further, limited autoignition delay
measurements exist for methane-based blends composed with
more than 15% additive. The current study investigates the igni-
tion of methane-based fuel blends containing 0–100% additive
ethane and propane, as well as ternary combinations of methane,
ethane, and propane. The collected data spans the equivalence
ratio range from 0.5 to 1.25, providing a comprehensive matrix of
fuel blends and conditions. This paper presents those measure-
ments beginning with a description of the experimental ﬂow reac-
tor, followed by a discussion on the analysis for determining au-
toignition delay time. The experimental results are presented with
Arrhenius correlations that were derived from the data. The igni-
tion delay time data are also compared with the predictions from
plug ﬂow reactor models using a detailed chemical kinetics
mechanism.
2
Measurement of Ignition Delay Time
The autoignition delay time measurement apparatus is a modi-
ﬁed version of a rig, previously used to measure autoignition de-
lay times of kerosene-type fuels and is described in detail else-
where 27,28. A schematic of the experimental system is
provided in Fig. 3. The ﬂow reactor apparatus consisted of a fuel
preheating system, an air preheating system, a premixing section,
and a test section. Preheated fuel was injected into a hot air
stream, and after rapid mixing, the fuel/air mixture ignited at some
distance down the test section of the ﬂow reactor. The time was
recorded by the data acquisition system when the valve control-
ling fuel injection was opened and referenced as the fuel injection
time. The time at which chemiluminescence emissions excitation
of CH radicals were detected using a photomultiplier tube

title: Combustion Science and Engineering, Inc.,
text:
PMT was monitored and used to indicate the ignition of fuel-air
mixture. The time difference between when the fuel was injected
into the air stream and when the fuel and air mixture ignited was
deﬁned as the ignition delay time. A typical ignition event is
shown in Fig. 4.
The difﬁculties of making autoignition delay time measure-
ments in ﬂow reactor apparatus include achieving “instantaneous”
mixing of fuel and air, and a zero temperature gradient at the
entrance to the test section to maintain ideal plug-ﬂow conditions.
In the current experiments, a swirler, followed by an expanding
duct, was used to rapidly mix the fuel and air upstream of the ﬂow
reactor 27. However, the mixing of the fuel and air still has a
ﬁnite mixing time-scale. It was demonstrated that chemical and
physical mixing effects upstream of the ﬂow reactor can be ac-
counted for by modeling the premixing section with a chemical
reactor network of ideal reactors 29. Therefore, in the present
work, experiments were performed with ethylene to characterize
the physical and chemical mixing effects in the premixing section
on ignition delay time measurements.
A chemical reactor model CRM, consisting of a large number
of perfectly stirred reactors with inﬁnitely small volumes in a
series, was developed, based on the geometry and ﬂow conditions
in the transfer lines and premixing section to account for the mix-
ing effects on the ignition delay time measurements. The mixing
time upstream of the test section of the ﬂow reactor was therefore
numerically computed as the sum of the residence times of the
series of perfectly stirred reactors representing the premixing sec-
tion. The mixing time was then subtracted from the experimental
measurements to obtain autoignition delay time. The computed
mixing time-scale in the premixing zone ranged between 125 ms
and 205 ms for various temperatures and ﬂow rates studied.
The accuracy of several available chemical kinetics mecha-
nisms in predicting existing

title: Combustion Science and Engineering, Inc.,
text:
results reported herein. Predictions of autoignition delay time us-
ing the detailed C1-C3 kinetics mechanism developed by Petersen
et al. 1 are presented for all fuels in the current study.
Figure 7 shows ignition delay time data for methane from the
present work. Because only two test temperatures resulted in suc-
cessful autoignition events for the methane experiments, it is dif-
ﬁcult to draw any meaningful conclusions about the resulting rate
parameters. Ea for methane oxidation in the temperature regime
studied was found to be 46.6 kcal/mol. Studies available in the
literature at various temperature and pressure regimes reported
activation energies ranging from 19 kcal/mol to 51.8 kcal/mol
5,11,13,15,23,41.
Figure 7 compares the methane ignition delay time data ob-
tained in the current work with the detailed kinetics model predic-
tions. The predictions made by the GRI 3.0 mechanism have rea-
sonable agreement with the experimental results. The kinetics
mechanism of Petersen et al. 1 predicted the measurements
fairly well at 0.5 equivalence ratio, but overpredicted the mea-
sured autoignition delay times of the stoichiometric mixture.
Similar autoignition delay time measurements were made for
pure ethane and propane fuels. Figures 8 and 9 show the ignition
delay time data for ethane and propane, respectively, compared
with Arrhenius correlations, as well as detailed kinetics model
predictions of Petersen et al. 1. Figure 10 compares the propane/
air ignition delay time data of the current work with the data from
the literature obtained at 1 atm 42,43. The detailed kinetics
mechanism of Petersen et al. 1 predicts a lower ignition delay
time than the experimental data below 1100 K, while the model
agrees with the experimental data of Kim et al. 43 fairly well, as
shown in Fig. 10.
The regression analysis, which is plotted with the data in Figs.
8 and 9, computed Ea for ethane 40.0 kcal/mol slightly larger
than for propane 38.5 kcal/mol. The H-atom abstraction from
secondary C-H sites requires less energy than from primary sites.
Therefore, ethane and propane fuels provide lower Ea for ignition
than methane, which solely consists of primary C-H sites. deVries
et al. 18 studied ethane ignition in the temperature range of 1218
K to 1860 K at pressures of 0.57 atm to 3 atm and for equivalence
ratios ranging from 0.5 to 2. The activation energy reported by
deVries et al. 18 for ethane oxidation was 39.6 kcal/mol, which
agrees well with the current study. For propane ignition, Cadman
et al. 21 reports an activation energy of 9.1 kcal/mol at 5 atm,

25.8 kcal/mol for pressures of 5 atm, =0.5 with T from 998 K to
1357 K.
The ethane and propane measurements shown in Figs. 8 and 9
suggest a decrease in Ea at T1000 K. This trend is most promi-
nent for ethane at the leaner equivalence ratios. Other researchers
have noted this trend at lower temperatures near 800 K for el-
evated pressures 20 atm and it has been associated with a nega-
tive temperature coefﬁcient for hydrocarbon ignition 16. The
predictions of autoignition delay time by the kinetics mechanism
of Petersen et al. 1 do not capture this reduction in effective Ea
but rather predict an effectively constant Ea over the entire range
of temperatures studied, as indicated in Figs. 8 and 9. Further, the
mechanism underpredicts the experimental measurements of au-
toignition delay time for both ethane and propane.
The autoignition delay times measured for pure ethane and pro-
pane show a slight decrease when increasing the equivalence ratio
from 0.5 to 1.0. The predictions of Petersen et al. 1 suggest that
this trend is followed only below a certain temperature, and that as
temperature increases well above 1000 K, increasing equivalence
ratio will increase autoignition delay time. This predicted trend at
high temperatures was not observed in the experiments.
This shifting trend with temperature regime was experimentally
veriﬁed by Healy et al. 25, who studied methane/propane fuel
mixtures. The authors have observed that fuel-lean mixtures react
faster than fuel-rich mixtures at high temperatures, and fuel-rich
mixtures react faster than fuel-lean mixtures at lower tempera-
tures. The authors attribute this phenomenon to the fact that at
lower temperatures, chain-branching is dependant on fuel concen-
tration. Therefore fuel-rich mixtures will exhibit faster kinetics,
resulting in quicker autoignition. At higher temperatures, the H
+O2→O+OH reaction becomes the rate-limiting chain-branching
reaction, and thus, reactivity is more strongly dependent on O2
than on CxHy. Therefore, mixtures with lower  and therefore
higher O2 will have shorter autoignition delay time.
The temperature at which the shift in controlling kinetics occurs
has been shown to be highly dependent on pressure. Healy et al.
25 found very good agreement of their data, which was collected
at pressures ranging from 10 atm to 30 atm, with predictions made
using the kinetics mechanism of Petersen et al. 1. The authors
found that as pressure increases, the critical temperature for the
shift in controlling kinetics increases. The atmospheric pressure
measurements made in the current study, however, do not agree
well with predictions made by this kinetics mechanism. The ki-
netics mechanism of Petersen et al. 1 predicts the critical tem-
perature for a shift in the controlling kinetics at 1112 K for ethane
and 1025 K for propane at atmospheric pressure. For all tempera-
ture conditions experimentally studied for methane, ethane, and
propane autoignition, it was found that increasing  reduced au-
toignition delay time, indicating that the experiments were per-
formed at temperatures below the critical temperature associated
with the kinetics regime shift at atmospheric pressure. The mea-
surements made in the current study can therefore provide critical
validation data for further development of the kinetics mecha-
nisms at atmospheric pressure.
4
Autoignition Delay Time Measurements of Binary
Methane-Based Fuel Mixtures
Autoignition delay times were measured for binary mixtures of
methane/ethane and methane/propane in air. The following binary
compositions were investigated at equivalence ratios of 0.5 and
1.0: 95/5, 90/10, 75/25, 50/50, and 25/75. Figures 11 and 12 show
the measured autoignition delay times of the binary methane fuel
blends, as well as Arrhenius ﬁts to the data of the form shown in
Eq. 2. The mole fractions of methane and ethane used to gener-
ate the curve using the Arrhenius correlation in Fig. 11 were
0.0801 and 0.0089, respectively. The mole fractions of methane

et al. 1 are presented for all fuels in the current study.
Figure 7 shows ignition delay time data for methane from the
present work. Because only two test temperatures resulted in suc-
cessful autoignition events for the methane experiments, it is dif-
ﬁcult to draw any meaningful conclusions about the resulting rate
parameters. Ea for methane oxidation in the temperature regime
studied was found to be 46.6 kcal/mol. Studies available in the
literature at various temperature and pressure regimes reported
activation energies ranging from 19 kcal/mol to 51.8 kcal/mol
5,11,13,15,23,41.
Figure 7 compares the methane ignition delay time data ob-
tained in the current work with the detailed kinetics model predic-
tions. The predictions made by the GRI 3.0 mechanism have rea-
sonable agreement with the experimental results. The kinetics
mechanism of Petersen et al. 1 predicted the measurements
fairly well at 0.5 equivalence ratio, but overpredicted the mea-
sured autoignition delay times of the stoichiometric mixture.
Similar autoignition delay time measurements were made for
pure ethane and propane fuels. Figures 8 and 9 show the ignition
delay time data for ethane and propane, respectively, compared
with Arrhenius correlations, as well as detailed kinetics model
predictions of Petersen et al. 1. Figure 10 compares the propane/
air ignition delay time data of the current work with the data from
the literature obtained at 1 atm 42,43. The detailed kinetics
mechanism of Petersen et al. 1 predicts a lower ignition delay
time than the experimental data below 1100 K, while the model
agrees with the experimental data of Kim et al. 43 fairly well, as
shown in Fig. 10.
The regression analysis, which is plotted with the data in Figs.
8 and 9, computed Ea for ethane 40.0 kcal/mol slightly larger
than for propane 38.5 kcal/mol. The H-

text:
=1 with T from 850 K to 1280 K, and an activation energy of
25.8 kcal/mol for pressures of 5 atm, =0.5 with T from 998 K to
1357 K.
The ethane and propane measurements shown in Figs. 8 and 9
suggest a decrease in Ea at T1000 K. This trend is most promi-
nent for ethane at the leaner equivalence ratios. Other researchers
have noted this trend at lower temperatures near 800 K for el-
evated pressures 20 atm and it has been associated with a nega-
tive temperature coefﬁcient for hydrocarbon ignition 16. The
predictions of autoignition delay time by the kinetics mechanism
of Petersen et al. 1 do not capture this reduction in effective Ea
but rather predict an effectively constant Ea over the entire range
of temperatures studied, as indicated in Figs. 8 and 9. Further, the
mechanism underpredicts the experimental measurements of au-
toignition delay time for both ethane and propane.
The autoignition delay times measured for pure ethane and pro-
pane show a slight decrease when increasing the equivalence ratio
from 0.5 to 1.0. The predictions of Petersen et al. 1 suggest that
this trend is followed only below a certain temperature, and that as
temperature increases well above 1000 K, increasing equivalence
ratio will increase autoignition delay time. This predicted trend at
high temperatures was not observed in the experiments.
This shifting trend with temperature regime was experimentally
veriﬁed by Healy et al. 25, who studied methane/propane fuel
mixtures. The authors have observed that fuel-lean mixtures react
faster than fuel-rich mixtures at high temperatures, and fuel-rich
mixtures react faster than fuel-lean mixtures at lower tempera-
tures. The authors attribute this phenomenon to the fact that at
lower temperatures, chain-branching is dependant on fuel concen-
tration. Therefore fuel-rich mixtures will exhibit faster kinetics,
resulting in quicker autoignition. At higher temperatures, the H
+O2→O+OH reaction becomes the rate-limiting chain-branchi

relation in Fig. 12 were 0.0393 and 0.0044, respectively

Several researchers derived Arrhenius expressions for methane
oxidation, but only Spadacccini and Colket 7 attempted to ac-
count for the effects of hydrocarbon additives in the methane-
based fuel mixtures. In their correlation, all non-methane hydro-
carbons are lumped together in one term. In the current study,
Arrhenius ﬁts are made for both methane/ethane and methane/
propane binary fuel mixture oxidation. Figures 11 and 12 also
compare the data to predictions by the kinetics mechanism of
Petersen et al. 1 for fuel mixtures composed of 90% methane
and 10% ethane or propane.
Figure 13 shows how increasing the higher hydrocarbon con-
centration in a methane-based fuel blend impacts autoignition de-
lay time. Methane/propane binary mixture autoignition delay time
is presented as a function of propane mole fraction in the fuel. It
can be noted that with less than 10 mol % addition of ethane or
propane to methane, methane autoignition delay times can be re-
duced signiﬁcantly. This trend can be seen most clearly in the data
obtained at 1085 K. A conclusion cannot be made regarding the
effect of small additions of ethane or propane to methane at the
lower temperatures because autoignition was not achievable in the

title: Combustion Science and Engineering, Inc.,
text:
experimental reactor for the residence times of this study. For the
higher test temperatures, reductions in methane autoignition delay
time of up to 50% were observed in the mixtures composed of up
to 10% ethane or propane by volume. On the other hand, promo-
tional effects of ethane and propane on methane autoignition
greatly diminish above 20% additions.
Figure 13 also shows the signiﬁcant increase in autoignition
delay time of pure propane with the addition of methane. This
trend was also seen in the addition of methane to pure ethane.
Increases in autoignition delay times of ethane and propane of up
to 50% were observed with the addition of methane, making the
resulting concentration of the methane blend 25% methane/75%
ethane or propane.
Differences in autoignition delay times were observed if ethane
or propane was an additive; ethane addition tended to reduce au-
toignition delay times of methane blends more than propane. The
majority of tests indicate that ethane addition typically reduced
methane autoignition delay time about 10% more than that of
propane addition. While some results show this difference as high
as 40%, few results show similar reductions with either ethane or
propane, and even some results show larger reductions with pro-
pane than ethane.
Very few researchers have derived global rate parameters for
the ignition of the fuel blends studied. Only Petersen et al. 5
derived global activation energies for the oxidation of the fuel
blends. In their study, an 80% methane/20% propane mixture was
found to have an effective activation energy of 41.9 kcal/mol be-
tween 1189 K and 1615 K at 12.2 atm. This agrees with Ea
=41.8 kcal/mol, derived from the data collected in the current
study. Petersen et al. 5 also found an effective activation energy
of 42.1 kcal/mol for 90% methane/10% ethane mixture oxidation,
which agrees with Ea=42.2 kcal/mol, derived from the data col-
lected in the current study. The derived Ea for the methane fuel
blends was found to be lower than Ea for pure methane, but higher
than Ea for ethane or propane pure fuel additive.
The kinetics mechanism of Petersen et al. 1 predicted a very
similar autoignition delay time for the methane/ethane and
methane/propane blends. The mechanism overpredicted the autoi-
gnition delay times of methane, but underpredicted the autoigni-
tion delay times of pure ethane and propane. However, the mecha-
nism performed very well in predicting autoignition delay times
of methane blends containing low concentrations of ethane and
propane. The mechanism of Petersen et al. 1 predicts a reduction
in autoignition delay time with a reduction in equivalence ratio for
the conditions of the binary mixtures studied. This trend was again
evident in the predictions made above a critical temperature for
pure ethane and propane autoignition.
The kinetics mechanism predicts a shift in the controlling ki-
netics of methane/ethane fuel blend oxidation near 930 K. This
prediction was not veriﬁed by the experiments, as fuel-rich
methane/ethane mixtures were found to have shorter autoignition
delay times than fuel-lean mixtures for the entire temperature
range investigated. A similar trend was predicted for methane/
propane mixtures. Figure 12 indicates that, for the conditions
studied, the mechanism predicts a reduction in methane/propane
mixture autoignition delay time with decreasing equivalence ratio.
It was found in the current study that the critical temperature for a
shift in the controlling kinetics, indicated by the effect of equiva-
lence ratio on ignition delay time, was lower than the predictions
by this mechanism. The data collected in the current study will
provide a basis for future kinetics model validation in the atmo-
spheric pressure regime.
5
Autoignition Delay Time Measurements of Ternary
Methane-Based Fuel Mixtures
Methane-based ternary blends containing both ethane and pro-
pane were studied to investigate the effect of multiple hydrocar-
bon addition on methane autoignition delay time to provide fur-
ther validation data for natural gas chemical kinetics mechanisms.
Two ternary fuel mixtures were studied at =0.5 and 1.0: mixture
1: 50% methane, 25% ethane, and 25% propane; and mixture 2:
85% methane, 10% ethane, and 5% propane. Figure 14 shows the

measured autoignition delay times for these mixtures, as well as
predictions of autoignition delay time made by the kinetics
mechanism of Petersen et al. 1. As with the pure fuels and binary
methane fuel blends, autoignition delay times of the ternary meth-
ane fuel blends exhibited the same trends with temperature and
equivalence ratio. Figure 14 shows the same near exponential de-
crease in autoignition delay time with increased temperature. And
again, this reduction in autoignition delay time decreases at higher
temperature, again, more evidently for leaner mixtures.
Autoignition delay times of mixture 1 were signiﬁcantly up to
50% shorter than those of mixture 2 at the same conditions. This
was expected as mixture 1 had signiﬁcantly more ethane and pro-
pane added to the methane than in mixture 2. Addition of both
ethane and propane had been observed to decrease the autoigni-
tion delay time of methane. This data will serve to improve the
accuracy of chemical kinetics tools in predicting typical natural
gas ignition behavior.
In comparing the autoignition delay times of binary mixtures
made up of 50% methane and ternary mixture 1 50% methane/
25% ethane/25% propane, the values of autoignition delay times
for ternary mixture 1 fall between the autoignition delay times of
a binary methane/ethane mixture and a binary methane/propane
mixture. While the autoignition delay times measured for all three
of these mixtures are within about 10% of one another for each
test temperature, the results indicate that the promotional effects
of ethane and propane on methane autoignition are individually
signiﬁcant.
Conversely to what was seen in the binary methane/ethane fuel
blends, the kinetics mechanism of Petersen et al. 1 underpre-
dicted the autoignition delay times measured for the ternary
methane-based fuels. This indicates that perhaps the mechanism
overemphasizes the synergetic effect of natural gas surrogate com-
ponents on reducing methane autoignition delay time at atmo-
spheric pressure. As was seen in the autoignition delay time mea-
surements of the pure fuels, and to a lesser extent, the binary
methane blends, a very signiﬁcant asymptotic trend of autoigni-
tion delay time reduction was evident at the higher temperatures
for the ternary fuel blends. This trend was not captured by the
mechanism predictions.
The temperature at which the shift in controlling kinetics occurs
was predicted to be 1100 K for mixture 1 and 1075 K for mixture
2 by the mechanism developed by Petersen et al. 1. As was
found for the pure fuel and binary methane mixtures studied, au-
toignition delay times decreased with increasing equivalence ratio
for the range of temperatures studied for the ternary mixtures as
well. Therefore, again, it appears that the kinetics mechanism un-
derpredicts this critical temperature for the mixtures and condi-
tions studied.

experimental autoignition delay time data reported in the literature
for the fuels studied herein has been collected at elevated pres-
sures and temperatures using shock tubes and rapid compression
machines. The measurements made at low to intermediate tem-
perature and atmospheric pressure ﬁll some regions in the param-
eter space of the published autoignition delay time of natural gas
fuels where previous data was sparse. Important trends of reduc-
tions in autoignition delay times of the fuels and fuel blends with
increasing temperature and equivalence ratio were observed. Fur-

ther, a reduction in the decrease of autoignition delay times at
higher temperatures was observed for the pure fuels and fuel
blends studied.
Arrhenius rate expressions providing global activation energies
for the oxidation of the pure alkanes and methane fuel blends have
been reported. Reductions in activation energy of methane oxida-
tion were observed with the addition of both ethane and propane.
Further, the trends observed in this study indicate that addition of
ethane and propane to methane has a very signiﬁcant promotional
effect on methane autoignition, as found by other researchers
9,24. The relative reactivity of methane, ethane, and propane
was determined, and global activation energies of the oxidation of
these pure fuels and mixtures of these fuels were derived. For the
conditions studied, methane oxidation activation energy was
found to be 46.6 kcal/mol. It was found that both ethane and
propane have very similar oxidation activation energies 40.0
kcal/mol and 38.5 kcal/mol, respectively. As additives to meth-
ane, both ethane and propane have the effect of reducing oxidation
activation energy. Binary methane/ethane fuels were found to
have oxidation activation energy of 42.2 kcal/mol and binary
methane/propane fuels of 41.8 kcal/mol. Differences in autoigni-
tion delay times were observed for ethane versus propane addi-
tives; ethane addition tended to reduce autoignition delay times of
methane blends about 10% further than propane.
An important ﬁnding, because of its application to practical
natural gas fuels, was that the most dramatic decrease 30–50%
in autoignition delay time of binary, methane-based fuels occurs
with the addition of 5–10% of ethane or propane. Further addition
of ethane or propane continued to reduce autoignition delay time,
but not as dramatically. This trend was found for all binary mix-
tures of methane/ethane and methane/propane.
This study indicates that current kinetics predictive tools seem
to capture the promotional effects of ethane and propane indi-
vidual additives on methane autoignition, but not the effect of
combinations of ethane and propane on methane autoignition de-
lay time adequately. Further, the data collected indicate that for
the temperature range studied 930–1140 K, autoignition delay
times of methane, ethane, and propane pure fuels and mixtures
decrease with increasing equivalence ratio at atmospheric pres-
sure. This is not captured by the current kinetics mechanisms,
which predict a shift in the controlling oxidation kinetics at atmo-
spheric pressure in the temperature range studied. Additional
mechanism development, inclusive of the data collected herein, is
needed to better model oxidation of natural gas fuel blends. Inter-
esting trends of increased autoignition delay time of methane-
based fuel blends were seen with CO2 addition; however, because
such little data was collected in this area, further study is required
to draw signiﬁcant conclusions about the effect of a fuel’s CO2
concentration on its autoignition delay time.

periments: Brent Turner, Casey Fuller, Nasir Hussain, and Chris
Nolan.
